### Way to reproduce:

from skimage import morphology
import cv2
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from skimage.util import invert
import numpy as np
file=r"D:\0Temp\jiangxiy\split\temp_0.tif"
file_ex=r"D:\0Temp\jiangxiy\split\temp_0_skel.tif"
imgGray = cv2.imread(file, flags=0)  # flags=0 灰度图像

ret, imgBin = cv2.threshold(imgGray, 0, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY)  # 二值化处理
imgBin[imgBin==255] = 1

skeleton01 = morphology.skeletonize(imgBin)
skeleton = skeleton01.astype(np.uint8) * 255
cv2.imwrite(file_ex,skeleton)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.subplot(121), plt.axis('off'), plt.title("origin")
plt.imshow(imgBin, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
plt.subplot(122), plt.title("skeleton"), plt.axis('off')
plt.imshow(skeleton, cmap='gray', vmin=0, vmax=255)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/pulls/comments/1782828428","pull_request_review_id":2340300344,"id":1782828428,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAB6-0c5qQ82M","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,127 @@
+import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+

Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

+import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+
+@lru_cache
+def all_backends():
+    """List all installed backends and information about them"""
+    backends = {}
+    backends_ = _entry_points(group="skimage_backends")
+    backend_infos = _entry_points(group="skimage_backend_infos")
+
+    for backend in backends_:
+        backends[backend.name] = {"implementation": backend}
+        try:
+            info = backend_infos[backend.name]
+            # Double () to load and then call the backend information function
+            backends[backend.name]["info"] = info.load()()
+        except KeyError:
+            pass
+
+    return backends
+
+
+def dispatchable(func):
+    """Mark a function as dispatchable.
+
+    When a decorated function is called the installed backends are
+    searched for an implementation. If no backend implements the function
+    then the scikit-image implementation is used.
+    """
+    func_name = func.__name__
+    func_module = func.__module__
+
+    # If no backends are installed at all or dispatching is disabled,
+    # return the original function. This way people who don't care about it
+    # don't see anything related to dispatching
+    if disable_dispatching() or not all_backends():
+        return func
+
+    @functools.wraps(func)
+    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
+        # Backends are tried in alphabetical order, this makes things
+        # predictable and stable across runs. Might need a better solution
+        # when it becomes common that users have more than one backend
+        # that would accept a call.
+        for name in sorted(all_backends()):
+            backend = all_backends()[name]
+            # If we have a BackendInformation object we check if the
+            # function we are looking for is implemented in the backend
+            if "info" in backend:
+                if (
+                    f"{func_module}.{func_name}"
+                    not in backend["info"].supported_functions
+                ):
+                    continue
+
+            backend_impl = backend["implementation"].load()
+
+            # Allow the backend to accept/reject a call based on the function
+            # name and the values of the arguments
+            wants_it = backend_impl.can_has(
+                f"{func_module}.{func_name}", *args, **kwargs
+            )","path":"skimage/util/_backends.py","commit_id":"78571c368f6c30eea77f3f5497a99b9d48d2708b","original_commit_id":"12864005faae3e64cbc08a3d9f3e5f9264d040f5","user":{"login":"lagru","id":20140352,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwMTQwMzUy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20140352?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru","html_url":"https://github.com/lagru","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Would align with the [Entry point spec](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/entry-points/). :+1: on using `module:func`.

Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)

- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

+import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+
+@lru_cache
+def all_backends():
+    """List all installed backends and information about them"""
+    backends = {}
+    backends_ = _entry_points(group="skimage_backends")
+    backend_infos = _entry_points(group="skimage_backend_infos")
+
+    for backend in backends_:
+        backends[backend.name] = {"implementation": backend}
+        try:
+            info = backend_infos[backend.name]
+            # Double () to load and then call the backend information function
+            backends[backend.name]["info"] = info.load()()
+        except KeyError:
+            pass
+
+    return backends
+
+
+def dispatchable(func):
+    """Mark a function as dispatchable.
+
+    When a decorated function is called the installed backends are
+    searched for an implementation. If no backend implements the function
+    then the scikit-image implementation is used.
+    """
+    func_name = func.__name__
+    func_module = func.__module__
+
+    # If no backends are installed at all or dispatching is disabled,
+    # return the original function. This way people who don't care about it
+    # don't see anything related to dispatching
+    if disable_dispatching() or not all_backends():
+        return func
+
+    @functools.wraps(func)
+    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
+        # Backends are tried in alphabetical order, this makes things
+        # predictable and stable across runs. Might need a better solution
+        # when it becomes common that users have more than one backend
+        # that would accept a call.
+        for name in sorted(all_backends()):
+            backend = all_backends()[name]
+            # If we have a BackendInformation object we check if the
+            # function we are looking for is implemented in the backend
+            if "info" in backend:
+                if (
+                    f"{func_module}.{func_name}"
+                    not in backend["info"].supported_functions
+                ):
+                    continue
+
+            backend_impl = backend["implementation"].load()
+
+            # Allow the backend to accept/reject a call based on the function
+            # name and the values of the arguments
+            wants_it = backend_impl.can_has(
+                f"{func_module}.{func_name}", *args, **kwargs
+            )
+            if not wants_it:
+                continue
+
+            # can_has("foo", ...) might be True, but the function might not actually
+            # be implemented in the backend
+            func_impl = backend_impl.get_implementation(f"{func_module}.{func_name}")
+            if func_impl is not None:
+                warnings.warn(
+                    f"Call to '{func.__module__}.{func_name}' was dispatched to"
+                    f" the '{name}' backend. Set SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING=1 to"
+                    " disable this.",
+                    DispatchNotification,
+                    # XXX from where should this warning originate?
+                    # XXX from where the function that was dispatched was called?
+                    # XXX or from where the user called a function that called
+                    # XXX a function that was dispatched?
+                    stacklevel=2,
+                )
+                return func_impl(*args, **kwargs)
+
+        else:
+            return func(*args, **kwargs)
+
+    return wrapper
+
+
+class BackendInformation:
+    """Information about a backend
+
+    A backend that wants to provide additional information about itself
+    should return an instance of this from its information entry-point.
+    """
+
+    def __init__(self, supported_functions):
+        self.supported_functions = supported_functions
+
+

Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do

* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors

Description:

Dear scikit-image team,

I hope this message finds you well.

I am writing to express my admiration for the scikit-image project and its incredible contributions to the field of image processing. Due to restrictions with internet access in China, it can sometimes be challenging to access your official website, especially for users within the country who rely on your documentation and resources for their work.

I would like to ask for your permission to deploy a mirror or localized version of your website in China. The goal is to ensure that more developers and researchers in China can easily access scikit-image’s documentation and resources. Of course, I would comply with your guidelines and ensure that the deployment accurately reflects your content and branding.

Please let me know your thoughts on this proposal and if there are any specific steps or requirements I should follow.

Thank you for your time, and I look forward to your response.
Best regards","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7559/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7559/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/comments/2386080129","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7559#issuecomment-2386080129","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7559","id":2386080129,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAB6-0c6OOLGB","user":{"login":"lagru","id":20140352,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwMTQwMzUy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20140352?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru","html_url":"https://github.com/lagru","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-01T14:09:29Z","updated_at":"2024-10-01T14:09:29Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks for checking in with us and offering! +import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+

Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

backend","user":{"login":"betatim","id":1448859,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NDg4NTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1448859?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim","html_url":"https://github.com/betatim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

+import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+

backend","user":{"login":"betatim","id":1448859,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NDg4NTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1448859?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim","html_url":"https://github.com/betatim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

I was trying to think about reasons, for why a user might want to control the colors over random ones. One use case I came up with is, that users might want to color code machtes by some property. So this feature is less about convenience and doing something for the user and more about making it even possible. 

To me, cycling seemed not a good fit for multiple reasons:
- It's "easy" to implement from a user side and the above API with `itertools.cylce`.
- I actually think cycling makes for a more complicated API. We would have to deal gracefully with an empty iterable (exception?) or an iterable with 1 item (warning?).
- I couldn't really think of good use cases for cycling or where it leads to better results than random coloring. I agree with @michaelbratsch sentiment, that it's harder to differentiate the lines with cycling through fewer colors.

So all in all, asking for a sequence of established length, seemed like the more API conservative and less assuming choice. 

Does that change your perspective? Am I missing {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/pulls/comments/1782977614","pull_request_review_id":2340552232,"id":1782977614,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAB6-0c5qRhRO","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,127 @@
+import functools
+from importlib.metadata import entry_points
+from functools import lru_cache
+import os
+import warnings
+
+
+def _entry_points(group):
+    # Support Python versions before 3.10, which do not let you
+    # filter groups directly.
+    all_entry_points = entry_points()
+    if hasattr(all_entry_points, "select"):
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.select(group=group)
+    else:
+        selected_entry_points = all_entry_points.get(group, ())
+    return selected_entry_points
+
+
+def disable_dispatching():
+    """Determine if dispatching has been disabled by the user"""
+    no_dispatching = os.environ.get("SKIMAGE_NO_DISPATCHING", False)
+    if no_dispatching == "1":
+        return True
+    else:
+        return False
+
+

backend","user":{"login":"betatim","id":1448859,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NDg4NTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1448859?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim","html_url":"https://github.com/betatim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

backend","user":{"login":"betatim","id":1448859,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0NDg4NTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1448859?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim","html_url":"https://github.com/betatim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/betatim/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [ ] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

`mask` parameter for `graycomatrix` function

- **New Feature**: Added a `mask` parameter to the `graycomatrix` function, enabling selective computation of the gray-level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM).
  
- **Functionality**: The `mask` allows users to define regions of interest within an image using a binary array, where computations are performed only on the unmasked areas.
  
- **Documentation & Tests**: Updated the function's documentation and modified `test_texture.py` to reflect the new `mask` parameter.

Bug","color":"e31c3d","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-03-23T10:02:11Z","updated_at":"2024-10-02T14:58:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description:

The purpose of a Laplace filter is to compute an approximation for the second derivative. Which means that at the position of a maximum, it should give us back a negative number. That is e.g. the behavior of the scipy implementation of a Laplace filter. The filter matrix in skimage is inverted.
Fixing this bug will break the workarounds some people might have used in the past (i.e. multiplying the skimage result with minus 1). 

### Way to reproduce:

```python
import numpy as np
import skimage as ski

from scipy import signal
maximum = np.outer(signal.windows.gaussian(5, 1)*10, signal.windows.gaussian(5,1))*10
np.set_printoptions(precision = 1)
print ('a 2D Gaussian maximum:\n',maximum)

laplace_result= ski.filters.laplace(maximum)
print('\nLaplace filter result:\n',laplace_result)


### the underlying reason is that the signs in the filter matrix are inverted
### as can be seen by a comparison with the scipy implementation
from scipy import ndimage

A = np.zeros((3,3))
A[1,1] = 1

laplace_filter_scipy = ndimage.laplace(A)
laplace_filter_skimage= ski.filters.laplace(A)

print('scipy:\n',laplace_filter_scipy)
print('\nskimage:\n',laplace_filter_skimage)
```

### Version information:

```Shell
3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]
Linux-6.7.9-1-default-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
scikit-image version: 0.22.0
numpy version: 1.26.0
```

However, with the default now, my gut feeling right now is to leave it as is. The definition we are using seems not entirely exotic and confusing. However, we should definitely document this definition in the docstrings of both affected functions. Bug","color":"e31c3d","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-03-23T10:02:11Z","updated_at":"2024-10-02T15:10:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Description:

The purpose of a Laplace filter is to compute an approximation for the second derivative. Which means that at the position of a maximum, it should give us back a negative number. That is e.g. the behavior of the scipy implementation of a Laplace filter. The filter matrix in skimage is inverted.
Fixing this bug will break the workarounds some people might have used in the past (i.e. multiplying the skimage result with minus 1). 

### Way to reproduce:

```python
import numpy as np
import skimage as ski

from scipy import signal
maximum = np.outer(signal.windows.gaussian(5, 1)*10, signal.windows.gaussian(5,1))*10
np.set_printoptions(precision = 1)
print ('a 2D Gaussian maximum:\n',maximum)

laplace_result= ski.filters.laplace(maximum)
print('\nLaplace filter result:\n',laplace_result)


### the underlying reason is that the signs in the filter matrix are inverted
### as can be seen by a comparison with the scipy implementation
from scipy import ndimage

A = np.zeros((3,3))
A[1,1] = 1

laplace_filter_scipy = ndimage.laplace(A)
laplace_filter_skimage= ski.filters.laplace(A)

print('scipy:\n',laplace_filter_scipy)
print('\nskimage:\n',laplace_filter_skimage)
```

### Version information:

```Shell
3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]
Linux-6.7.9-1-default-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
scikit-image version: 0.22.0
numpy version: 1.26.0
```

Description

* fix seed for `plot_matches`
* `matches_color` allows sequence of colors

Relates to: https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7455

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note
Allow passing a sequence of colors to the parameter `matches_color`
in `skimage.feature.plot_matched_features`.
```

```release-note
Make sure that `skimage.feature.plot_matched_features` uses
the same random colors, if `matches_color` isn't provided 
explicitly.

-def _trim_zeros(filt, trim=None):
+def _arg_trim_zeros(filt):
+    """Return indices of the first and last non-zero element.
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    filt : array_like
+        Input array.
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    start, stop : ndarray
+        Two arrays containing the indices of the first and last non-zero
+        element in each dimension.
+
+    See also
+    --------
+    trim_zeros
+
+    Examples
+    --------
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> _arg_trim_zeros(np.array([0, 0, 1, 1, 0]))
+    (array([2]), array([3]))
+    """
+    nonzero = np.argwhere(filt != 0)
+    if nonzero.size == 0:
+        start = stop = np.array([], dtype=np.intp)
+    else:
+        start = nonzero.min(axis=0)
+        stop = nonzero.max(axis=0)
+    return start, stop
+
+
+def _trim_zeros(filt, trim=None, axis=None):
     return (filt,)

Even if I do something about the internal call to `np.asarray` that converts the masked array back

```python
import numpy as np
b = np.array([[1, 1, 2, 3, 1, 1],
              [1, 1, 0, 3, 0, 1],
              [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]])
b_masked = np.ma.MaskedArray(b, mask=(b == 1))
np.trim_zeros(b_masked)
```
will still trim zeros as well, in this case the zero at `b[1, 4]`. It shouldn't in a case where we only want to trim ones.

The alternative I see is to make the currently internal function `_arg_trim_zeros` public. But might be another support for trimming nd-arrays with trim_zeros while preserving the old behavior for 1D input. The new parameter `axis` can specify a single dimension to be trimmed (reducing all other dimensions to the envelope of absolute values). If None or multiple values are specified, all or the selected dimensions are trimmed iteratively.

Additionally provide the `atol`, `rtol` and `return_lengths` parameters. The first two control what is considered  a "zero" to be trimmed, the latter provides the user with the on how much was trimmed.

I recently needed this behavior myself and think this makes the function applicable to more use cases.

This is currently marked as work in progress, as I wasn't sure about the new API. I tried to preserve the old API but I think it's worth having a discussion about refactoring and additional API changes. See also the review-comments support for trimming nd-arrays with trim_zeros while preserving the old behavior for 1D input. The new parameter `axis` can specify a single dimension to be trimmed (reducing all other dimensions to the envelope of absolute values). If None or multiple values are specified, all or the selected dimensions are trimmed iteratively.

Additionally provide the `atol`, `rtol` and `return_lengths` parameters. The first two control what is considered  a "zero" to be trimmed, the latter provides the user with the on how much was trimmed.

I recently needed this behavior myself and think this makes the function applicable to more use cases.

This is currently marked as work in progress, as I wasn't sure about the new API. I tried to preserve the old API but I think it's worth having a discussion about refactoring and additional API changes. -def _trim_zeros(filt, trim=None):
+def _arg_trim_zeros(filt):
+    """Return indices of the first and last non-zero element.
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    filt : array_like
+        Input array.
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    start, stop : ndarray
+        Two arrays containing the indices of the first and last non-zero
+        element in each dimension.
+
+    See also
+    --------
+    trim_zeros
+
+    Examples
+    --------
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> _arg_trim_zeros(np.array([0, 0, 1, 1, 0]))
+    (array([2]), array([3]))
+    """
+    nonzero = np.argwhere(filt != 0)
+    if nonzero.size == 0:
+        start = stop = np.array([], dtype=np.intp)
+    else:
+        start = nonzero.min(axis=0)
+        stop = nonzero.max(axis=0)
+    return start, stop
+
+
+def _trim_zeros(filt, trim=None, axis=None):
     return (filt,)

support for trimming nd-arrays with trim_zeros while preserving the old behavior for 1D input. The new parameter `axis` can specify a single dimension to be trimmed (reducing all other dimensions to the envelope of absolute values). If None or multiple values are specified, all or the selected dimensions are trimmed iteratively.

Additionally provide the `atol`, `rtol` and `return_lengths` parameters. The first two control what is considered  a "zero" to be trimmed, the latter provides the user with the on how much was trimmed.

I recently needed this behavior myself and think this makes the function applicable to more use cases.

This is currently marked as work in progress, as I wasn't sure about the new API. I tried to preserve the old API but I think it's worth having a discussion about refactoring and additional API changes. See also the review-comments Description:

The purpose of a Laplace filter is to compute an approximation for the second derivative. Which means that at the position of a maximum, it should give us back a negative number. That is e.g. the behavior of the scipy implementation of a Laplace filter. The filter matrix in skimage is inverted.
Fixing this bug will break the workarounds some people might have used in the past (i.e. multiplying the skimage result with minus 1). 

### Way to reproduce:

```python
import numpy as np
import skimage as ski

from scipy import signal
maximum = np.outer(signal.windows.gaussian(5, 1)*10, signal.windows.gaussian(5,1))*10
np.set_printoptions(precision = 1)
print ('a 2D Gaussian maximum:\n',maximum)

laplace_result= ski.filters.laplace(maximum)
print('\nLaplace filter result:\n',laplace_result)


### the underlying reason is that the signs in the filter matrix are inverted
### as can be seen by a comparison with the scipy implementation
from scipy import ndimage

A = np.zeros((3,3))
A[1,1] = 1

laplace_filter_scipy = ndimage.laplace(A)
laplace_filter_skimage= ski.filters.laplace(A)

print('scipy:\n',laplace_filter_scipy)
print('\nskimage:\n',laplace_filter_skimage)
```

### Version information:

```Shell
3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]
Linux-6.7.9-1-default-x86_64-with-glibc2.39
scikit-image version: 0.22.0
numpy version: 1.26.0
```

Even if a parameter is deprecated, a value passed to it must be preserved during the deprecation cycle! Othwerwise, we effectively remove the deprecated behavior before the cycle is complete.

This is only relevant if there's no replacement for the deprecated parameter. In that case, the given value preserved and passed to the replacing parameter, and the deprecated one can be overwritten with `DEPRECATED`.

E.g.
```python
from skimage._shared.utils import deprecate_parameter, DEPRECATED

@deprecate_parameter("a", start_version="X", stop_version="X+2")
def foo(a=DEPRECATED):
    if a is True:
        print("Can still use deprecated behavior!")

foo(True)
```
should warn **and** print but only warns on `main` because it silently replaces with `DEPRECATED`.

Thankfully, it doesn't look like we are currently using the decorator in a way that is affected by this bug. Discovered while preparing #7353.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note
During deprecation cycles, preserve the value of deprecated parameters
that don't have a new parameter as a replacement.

{"repository_id":2014929,"push_id":20543795880,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"8e37f77568b580be56424adc3ac4b5515da3ab2e","before":"ba34f70de098bd93bee34d655700e41b289ed888","commits":[{"sha":"8e37f77568b580be56424adc3ac4b5515da3ab2e","author":{"email":"3f8e01313814a03a9f317913ab169678ef0a55e5@mailbox.org","name":"Lars Grüter"},"message":"Preserve value of deprecated parameters (#7552)

Even if a parameter is deprecated, a value passed to it must be
preserved during the deprecation cycle! Othwerwise, we effectively
remove the deprecated behavior before the cycle is complete.

This is only relevant if there's no replacement for the deprecated
parameter. In that case, the given value preserved and passed to the
replacing parameter, and the deprecated one can be overwritten with
`DEPRECATED`.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/commits/8e37f77568b580be56424adc3ac4b5515da3ab2e"}]}

We have a rework of our footprint generating function on our [skimage2 agenda](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/wiki/API-changes-for-skimage2#proposed-api-changes-doable-independent-of-skimage2). This WIP starts that work by combining "rectangular" footprint generators (`square`, `rectangle`, `cube`) into a single `skimage.morphology.footprint_rectangle` with ND support. 

This is also an opportunity to change to `decomposition="sequence"` as the default as suggested in the agenda. Though, I'm not sure if we really want to do this. It's arguably a more unexpected form to represent the footprint for the sake of performance. Usually, we decide that trade-off in favor of simpler behavior.

I plan to do the same for "disk" like footprint generators in a separate PR to keep reviewing reasonable. 

I'm also playing around with a small wrapper class to help with the more complicated representation of decomposed footprints. I might move that into another PR depending on how this approach turns out.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

We have a rework of our footprint generating function on our [skimage2 agenda](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/wiki/API-changes-for-skimage2#proposed-api-changes-doable-independent-of-skimage2). This WIP starts that work by combining "rectangular" footprint generators (`square`, `rectangle`, `cube`) into a single `skimage.morphology.footprint_rectangle` with ND support. 

This is also an opportunity to change to `decomposition="sequence"` as the default as suggested in the agenda. Though, I'm not sure if we really want to do this. It's arguably a more unexpected form to represent the footprint for the sake of performance. Usually, we decide that trade-off in favor of simpler behavior.

I plan to do the same for "disk" like footprint generators in a separate PR to keep reviewing reasonable. 

I'm also playing around with a small wrapper class to help with the more complicated representation of decomposed footprints. I might move that into another PR depending on how this approach turns out.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

We have a rework of our footprint generating function on our [skimage2 agenda](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/wiki/API-changes-for-skimage2#proposed-api-changes-doable-independent-of-skimage2). This WIP starts that work by combining "rectangular" footprint generators (`square`, `rectangle`, `cube`) into a single `skimage.morphology.footprint_rectangle` with ND support. 

This is also an opportunity to change to `decomposition="sequence"` as the default as suggested in the agenda. Though, I'm not sure if we really want to do this. It's arguably a more unexpected form to represent the footprint for the sake of performance. Usually, we decide that trade-off in favor of simpler behavior.

I plan to do the same for "disk" like footprint generators in a separate PR to keep reviewing reasonable. 

I'm also playing around with a small wrapper class to help with the more complicated representation of decomposed footprints. I might move that into another PR depending on how this approach turns out.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Fixes #7543. Apparently, the minigallery directive ignores the currentmodule directive that sets a module context. To reference the appropriate minigallery object and for it to appear, we need the full string.

I believe this was broken (by me) in https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7486. 

We probably want to include this in the 0.25 release.

cc @jarrodmillman, @stefanv

Description

We have a rework of our footprint generating function on our [skimage2 agenda](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/wiki/API-changes-for-skimage2#proposed-api-changes-doable-independent-of-skimage2). This WIP starts that work by combining "rectangular" footprint generators (`square`, `rectangle`, `cube`) into a single `skimage.morphology.footprint_rectangle` with ND support. 

This is also an opportunity to change to `decomposition="sequence"` as the default as suggested in the agenda. Though, I'm not sure if we really want to do this. It's arguably a more unexpected form to represent the footprint for the sake of performance. Usually, we decide that trade-off in favor of simpler behavior.

I plan to do the same for "disk" like footprint generators in a separate PR to keep reviewing reasonable. 

I'm also playing around with a small wrapper class to help with the more complicated representation of decomposed footprints. I might move that into another PR depending on how this approach turns out.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

We have a rework of our footprint generating function on our [skimage2 agenda](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/wiki/API-changes-for-skimage2#proposed-api-changes-doable-independent-of-skimage2). This WIP starts that work by combining "rectangular" footprint generators (`square`, `rectangle`, `cube`) into a single `skimage.morphology.footprint_rectangle` with ND support. 

This is also an opportunity to change to `decomposition="sequence"` as the default as suggested in the agenda. Though, I'm not sure if we really want to do this. It's arguably a more unexpected form to represent the footprint for the sake of performance. Usually, we decide that trade-off in favor of simpler behavior.

I plan to do the same for "disk" like footprint generators in a separate PR to keep reviewing reasonable. 

I'm also playing around with a small wrapper class to help with the more complicated representation of decomposed footprints. I might move that into another PR depending on how this approach turns out.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note

```

Fixes #6465. When deserializing a RegionProperties object with pickle, [`__setstate__`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#object.__setstate__) is accessed before any other attributes like are available. This leads to a RecursionError in `__getattr__` which doesn't account for this and tries to call attributes such as `self._intensity_image`. We can guard against this in multiple ways. I chose a very specific one in this case which makes sure that the default AttributeError is provoked.

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note
Ensure that `RegionProperties` objects returned by `skimage.measure.regionprops`
can be deserialized with pickle.
```

Description
In latest version of RegionProperties class, __getattr__ is implemented. This function goes into recursive call when it is serialized and deserialized. while deserializing, __setstate__ is called before __init__. Since __setstate__ is not available, __getattr__ is called, and since __init__ has not yet been called, _extra_properties attribute is not defined which ends up calling __getattr__ multiple times until recursion limit is reached.       
<!--
(Note: for guidance on how to use `scikit-image`, please post instead on https://forum.image.sc/tag/scikit-image)
-->

## Way to reproduce
```python
import numpy as np
import os
from skimage import measure
import pickle

def create_fake_label_image():
    all_ones = np.full((5,5), 1, dtype=int)
    all_twos = np.full((5,5), 2, dtype=int)
    all_zero = np.full((5,5), 0, dtype=int)

    return np.concatenate((all_ones, all_zero, all_twos))

def regionProp_serialization_demo():

    #generate region props from fake data
    label_img = create_fake_label_image()
    regions = measure.regionprops(label_img)

    #pickle and unpickle
    pickled = pickle.dumps(regions[0])
    un_pickled = pickle.loads(pickled) #RecursionError here



if __name__ == "__main__":
    
    regionProp_serialization_demo()
```
## Output
```
File "array_reader.py", line 32, in <module>
    regionProp_serialization_demo()
  File "array_reader.py", line 26, in regionProp_serialization_demo
    un_pickled = pickle.loads(pickled) #RecursionError here
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  [Previous line repeated 994 more times]
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
```
## Workaround solution 
This is the workaround I am thinking to use for now. I am new to python, can someone please review and let me know if this is the best way or if there is any other better way. A cleaner solution in the package would be the best. 

Create a derived class that does not allow recursive call in __getattr__ and use the derived class in client code
```python
class SerializableRegionProperties(measure._regionprops.RegionProperties):

    def __init__(self, slice = None, label = None, label_image = None, intensity_image = None, cache_active = None,extra_properties=None):
        super().__init__(slice, label, label_image, intensity_image, cache_active, extra_properties = extra_properties)
        self._initialized = True
    
    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        if(attr == "_initialized" or not self._initialized):
            self.__init__()
        
        super().__getattr__(attr)
```

```python
3.8.10 (default, Jul  3 2022, 03:19:42) 
[GCC 9.3.0]
Linux-4.12.14-150.47-default-x86_64-with-glibc2.29
scikit-image version: 0.19.3
numpy version: 1.19.5
```

feature","color":"137c7b","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-09-01T08:02:13Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T19:14:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/pulls/7520","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7520","diff_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7520.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7520.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"## Description

This PR implements a backend dispatching mechanism. There is a basic backend https://github.com/betatim/scikit-image-backend-phony that you can use to experiment with the dispatching mechanism.

A backend has to provide an entry-point that points to a namespace that contains the functions which the backend implements. The namespace has to be structured like the scikit-image namespace.

In addition a backend can provide a second entry-point which provides information about the backend. For example which functions it implements. This entry-point has to work even if exotic hardware that the backend relies on is not available. For example so that the scikit-image docs building process can import the entry-point on a run of the mill CI instance.

The dispatching mechanism is similar to what is in #7513 and #7466

## To do
* [x] change to one `can_has` function per backend. Pass in function name, it can still distribute the work if it wants to.
* [x] function naming, should it be `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error` or `skimage.metrics.simple_metrics.mean_squared_error`? The latter seems nicer, but I don't know how to do it
* [ ] documentation for users
* [ ] documentation for backend authors
* [ ] discuss: what other information to store in the `BackendInformation` object? 
	* homepage URL
	* fancy name? (different from the name used in the entry point)
	* URL of a JSON file with up to date information (see "use javascript to fetch..." point above)
	* version compatibility info? Better expressed via `pyproject/toml`/dont allow the backend package to be installed together with an incompatible scikit-image version?

it-image/scikit-image/pull/7569.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"## Description

Fixes #6465. When deserializing a RegionProperties object with pickle, [`__setstate__`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#object.__setstate__) is accessed before any other attributes are available. This leads to a RecursionError in `__getattr__` which doesn't account for this and tries to call attributes such as `self._intensity_image`. We can guard against this in multiple ways. I chose a very specific one in this case which makes sure that the default AttributeError is provoked.

{"action":"closed","number":7569,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/pulls/7569","id":2109069978,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAB6-0c59tdqa","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7569","diff_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7569.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7569.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7569","number":7569,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Ensure that `RegionProperties` can be deserialized with pickle","user":{"login":"lagru","id":20140352,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwMTQwMzUy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20140352?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru","html_url":"https://github.com/lagru","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lagru/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"## Description

Fixes #6465. When deserializing a RegionProperties object with pickle, [`__setstate__`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#object.__setstate__) is accessed before any other attributes are available. This leads to a RecursionError in `__getattr__` which doesn't account for this and tries to call attributes such as `self._intensity_image`. We can guard against this in multiple ways. I chose a very specific one in this case which makes sure that the default AttributeError is provoked.

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note
Ensure that `RegionProperties` objects returned by `skimage.measure.regionprops`
can be deserialized with pickle.

Description
In latest version of RegionProperties class, __getattr__ is implemented. This function goes into recursive call when it is serialized and deserialized. while deserializing, __setstate__ is called before __init__. Since __setstate__ is not available, __getattr__ is called, and since __init__ has not yet been called, _extra_properties attribute is not defined which ends up calling __getattr__ multiple times until recursion limit is reached.       
<!--
(Note: for guidance on how to use `scikit-image`, please post instead on https://forum.image.sc/tag/scikit-image)
-->

## Way to reproduce
```python
import numpy as np
import os
from skimage import measure
import pickle

def create_fake_label_image():
    all_ones = np.full((5,5), 1, dtype=int)
    all_twos = np.full((5,5), 2, dtype=int)
    all_zero = np.full((5,5), 0, dtype=int)

    return np.concatenate((all_ones, all_zero, all_twos))

def regionProp_serialization_demo():

    #generate region props from fake data
    label_img = create_fake_label_image()
    regions = measure.regionprops(label_img)

    #pickle and unpickle
    pickled = pickle.dumps(regions[0])
    un_pickled = pickle.loads(pickled) #RecursionError here



if __name__ == "__main__":
    
    regionProp_serialization_demo()
```

File "array_reader.py", line 32, in <module>
    regionProp_serialization_demo()
  File "array_reader.py", line 26, in regionProp_serialization_demo
    un_pickled = pickle.loads(pickled) #RecursionError here
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/skimage/measure/_regionprops.py", line 323, in __getattr__
    if attr in self._extra_properties:
  [Previous line repeated 994 more times]
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
```
## Workaround solution 
This is the workaround I am thinking to use for now. I am new to python, can someone please review and let me know if this is the best way or if there is any other better way. A cleaner solution in the package would be the best. 

Create a derived class that does not allow recursive call in __getattr__ and use the derived class in client code
```python
class SerializableRegionProperties(measure._regionprops.RegionProperties):

    def __init__(self, slice = None, label = None, label_image = None, intensity_image = None, cache_active = None,extra_properties=None):
        super().__init__(slice, label, label_image, intensity_image, cache_active, extra_properties = extra_properties)
        self._initialized = True
    
    def __getattr__(self, attr):
        if(attr == "_initialized" or not self._initialized):
            self.__init__()
        
        super().__getattr__(attr)
```

When deserializing a RegionProperties object with pickle, `__setstate__`
[1] is accessed before any other attributes like are available. This
leads to an RecursionError in `__getattr__` which doesn't account for
this and tries to call attributes such as `self._intensity_image`. We
can guard against this in multiple ways. I chose a very specific one in
this case which makes sure that the default AttributeError is provoked
(gh-6465).

Description

Closes #6632 and closes #4240.
We could try fixing #6279 too, but it will need much more changes, including the heap.
It's not worth it, IMO, because their issue happens in a topology with tie-zones where the watershed solution is not unique and depends on **implementation details**. In the non-tie-zone case, it should be working (#4240).
I suggest closing it with won't fix.

One test had a similar problem to the abovementioned issues, so I did not implement any regression test and only updated the test. It previously expected a line from the watershed like #6632.

By the way, I could not find the minimum double value in cython. This could improve my PR.

## Checklist

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

Summarize the introduced changes in the code block below in one or a few sentences. The
summary will be included in the next release notes automatically:

```release-note
Fix the behavior of `skimage.segmentation.watershed` when
the markers don't align with local minima by making sure
every marker is evaluated before successive pixels.
```
```release-note
Fix edge case where setting `watershed_lines=True`
in `skimage.segmentation.watershed` resulted in an
incorrect solution.
```

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-09T11:35:08Z","updated_at":"2024-10-09T12:08:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider this docstring

```python
from numpydoc.docscrape import NumpyDocString

def foo(x):
    """Function docstring.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    x: int
        Some description
    """

NumpyDocString(foo.__doc__)["Parameters"]
```
returns
```
[Parameter(name='x: int', type='', desc=['Some description'])]
```

Notice how the type of the parameter is empty! While the [official syntax](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html#parameters) requires that the colon is preceded by a space, it's a typo that's very easy to miss and is very annoying in context of docstub. This case should be detected and either be warned about or handled
